Product.title: Product
Product.description: Represents a product available for selection in invoices.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the product was created.

category.description: Category of the invoice (BIGC, SPLZD, OTHER).

productName.description: Name of the product in the invoice. It could be a reference to Product entity's name or a new product name.

quantity.description: Quantity of the product in the invoice.

price.description: Unit price of the product in the invoice.

total.description: Total amount for the invoice item (calculated: quantity * price).

imageUrl.description: URL of the image associated with the invoice.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the invoice was created.

definition.description: Stores product information.  Includes `createdAt` for audit trails.
Stores invoice records. Includes `createdAt` for audit trails.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore structure is designed to manage products and invoices for the InvoiceFlow application. It prioritizes authorization independence and supports the application's core features, including dashboard summaries, invoice creation, product management, and reporting. The structure is segregated into distinct collections for products and invoices, ensuring a homogeneous security posture for each.

Products are stored in a simple collection, `/products`, and invoices are stored in `/invoices`. No authorization is required besides authentication since all users can access this data. This structure facilitates straightforward read operations (QAPs) for displaying products in the autocomplete dropdown and listing invoices in the table view. The decision to store invoices at the root allows for easy querying of all invoices without complex rule logic.

Specifically, the structure achieves Authorization Independence by avoiding any hierarchical dependencies. All necessary data for displaying and managing these entities exists within each document, removing the need for `get()` calls in the security rules.

QAPs are satisfied through structural segregation. List operations on `/products` and `/invoices` are inherently secure because the application logic does not require any filtering based on user roles or ownership within the rules themselves. Security rules can permit list operations based solely on authentication status, if required, without needing to inspect the content of the documents.
